questions.prompt: What is the fundamental structural difference between a greedy algorithm and a DP algorithm applied to the same class of problem?

options.text: Greedy commits immediately to the locally best choice at each step and never reconsiders it; DP considers multiple options and lets optimal substructure (via the recurrence) combine subproblem results to find the true optimum.
Greedy algorithms always run in exponential time, while DP algorithms always run in polynomial time.
Greedy and DP are two names for the same underlying technique, differing only in implementation style.
Greedy algorithms can only be applied to sorting problems, while DP applies to all other problem types.

questions.explanation: This is the chapter's core structural distinction: immediate, irrevocable commitment (greedy) versus considering and combining multiple branches (DP) — which is exactly why greedy is faster when correct (no need to explore alternatives) and silently wrong when the local choice forecloses a better global option. (b) is false; greedy algorithms are typically FASTER (often linear or n log n) precisely because they don't explore alternatives, and DP's complexity depends entirely on state space size, not a fixed relationship to greedy. (c) directly contradicts the chapter's central distinction — they are different techniques with different correctness guarantees, not stylistic variants of each other. (d) is a fabricated, overly narrow restriction; greedy applies to a range of problem types (scheduling, compression, graph algorithms like Kruskal's/Prim's from the DSA course) beyond sorting.

questions.prompt: Why is the exchange argument sufficient to prove the interval-scheduling greedy rule (always pick the earliest-ending remaining interval) is correct, rather than just empirically plausible?

options.text: The argument shows that ANY optimal solution can be transformed to agree with the greedy choice at every step without reducing the count — swapping an optimal solution's first interval for the earliest-ending one can only free up equal or more room for subsequent choices, never less — which generalizes to all inputs, not just tested cases.
It's sufficient because the code passed all example test cases the developer tried.
The exchange argument only proves correctness for inputs with fewer than 100 intervals.
It isn't actually a proof; interval scheduling's greedy correctness is still an open conjecture.

questions.explanation: This is precisely the distinction the chapter draws between 'passes examples' and 'proven correct' — the exchange argument is a general mathematical claim about ALL possible optimal solutions and all possible inputs, not a statement about specific test cases. (b) is exactly the weaker, insufficient standard the chapter explicitly contrasts against a real proof. (c) fabricates a scale limitation that doesn't exist in the actual mathematical argument, which holds for any n. (d) is false — interval scheduling's greedy correctness is a well-established, proven result, not an open question.

questions.prompt: Why does the 'highest value-per-weight ratio first' greedy rule fail for 0/1 Knapsack but succeed for the fractional knapsack variant (items can be split)?

options.text: 0/1 Knapsack's all-or-nothing constraint means committing to one high-ratio item can foreclose a better combination of other items entirely, with no way to partially back out; fractional knapsack removes that all-or-nothing commitment, which is exactly the property the counter-example exploited.
The greedy rule actually works correctly for both variants; the 0/1 version's apparent failures are implementation bugs, not an algorithmic limitation.
Fractional knapsack always has a smaller number of items than 0/1 knapsack, making greedy trivially correct.
0/1 Knapsack cannot be solved by any algorithm, greedy or otherwise.

questions.explanation: This is the chapter's precise diagnosis of why the SAME greedy rule flips from wrong to provably correct based on one constraint change: the ability to take a fraction of an item removes the irrevocable, all-or-nothing commitment that let a locally-best choice foreclose a globally-better combination in the 0/1 counter-example. (b) is the exact misconception the chapter's worked counter-example (A vs. B+C) directly disproves — this is a genuine algorithmic limitation, not a bug. (c) item count has nothing to do with why the correctness differs; the mechanism is about splittability, not quantity. (d) is false — 0/1 Knapsack is solved correctly by dynamic programming (chapter 6), just not by this particular greedy rule.

questions.prompt: What made the 0/1 Knapsack greedy failure 'silent,' as the chapter describes it, rather than something that would be caught immediately?

options.text: The algorithm runs without error and produces a plausible-looking number (10, in the worked example) with no indication that a better answer (12) exists — nothing about the execution signals that the local choice was suboptimal.
The algorithm throws a warning message whenever the greedy choice is suboptimal, but developers often ignore it.
The failure only manifests as a slower runtime, not an incorrect result.
The algorithm crashes with a stack trace that developers frequently misdiagnose.

questions.explanation: This is exactly the danger the chapter emphasizes about greedy failures generally: a wrong-but-plausible answer with zero runtime signal is far more dangerous than a crash, because nothing prompts investigation — the bug surfaces only when someone independently verifies the answer against a known-optimal solution. (b) no such warning mechanism exists in a standard greedy implementation; there's genuinely nothing to ignore. (c) directly contradicts the worked example, where the failure is a wrong VALUE (10 instead of 12), not a performance issue. (d) there is no crash in this scenario — the code executes cleanly and returns a wrong but well-formed answer, which is the entire point.

questions.prompt: Per the chapter, what is the practical, senior-level discipline to apply before committing to a greedy solution over a DP one?

options.text: Actively attempt to construct either an exchange argument (proving the local choice is always safe) or a counter-example (disproving it) — 'it passed my test cases' is explicitly insufficient.
Always prefer DP over greedy in every case, since DP is strictly more powerful and greedy should never be used.
Run the greedy algorithm on as many random test cases as possible; if none fail, correctness is established.
Check whether the problem statement uses the word 'optimal,' which determines whether greedy is appropriate.

questions.explanation: This is the chapter's explicit closing discipline, and it's specifically framed as an active, constructive check (attempt BOTH directions — proof or counter-example) rather than passive observation. (b) overcorrects — the chapter explicitly celebrates greedy's real, correct, production use (interval scheduling, Huffman coding) when a genuine proof exists; DP isn't universally preferred, it's used when greedy provably fails or can't be proven. (c) is exactly the 'passed my examples' standard the chapter explicitly rejects as insufficient — random testing can miss the specific structural cases where greedy fails, as the 0/1 knapsack example demonstrates. (d) is a fabricated, non-existent heuristic; word choice in a problem statement has no bearing on greedy's actual correctness.

questions.prompt: Huffman coding's greedy rule (repeatedly merge the two lowest-frequency nodes) is used in real production compression formats like DEFLATE. What makes this a legitimate, non-cautionary example of greedy in production, per the chapter?

options.text: The merge-two-smallest rule has its own exchange argument proving optimality (the two least-frequent symbols can always be placed at maximum tree depth without loss) — it's used in production BECAUSE a correctness proof exists, the same standard interval scheduling met.
It's used in production simply because it's fast, regardless of whether it's provably correct.
Huffman coding is actually a form of dynamic programming, not a true greedy algorithm.
Compression algorithms don't require correctness guarantees the way other algorithms do, since minor errors are acceptable in compressed data.

questions.explanation: This is the chapter's explicit point in citing Huffman coding: it's presented as a positive counterpart to the knapsack cautionary tale specifically because it also has a provable exchange argument, reinforcing that 'proof exists' — not 'seems to work' or 'is fast' — is the deciding factor for trusting greedy in production. (b) contradicts the chapter's entire thesis that speed alone doesn't justify trusting greedy without a correctness argument. (c) mischaracterizes the algorithm — it's explicitly described as greedy (immediate, irrevocable merge choices via a min-heap), not DP. (d) is dangerously false — compression MUST be exactly correct (lossless formats like DEFLATE require exact reconstruction); the chapter gives no indication that approximate correctness is acceptable here.
